attributes.stem:
A two-day-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with multiple blisters and erosions on his feet and legs that developed shortly after birth. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy. There is a family history of similar blistering in the infant's maternal uncle who died in early childhood. He has no other medical conditions and takes no medications. The parents have been applying vaseline to his ulcers and is using standard bath washes.

On examination, he appears mildly distressed though easily settled with his parents. His vital signs are within normal limits. There are multiple tense bullae and erosions on his feet and lower legs, with some areas of denuded skin. Nikolsky sign is positive. The rest of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with features suggestive of epidermolysis bullosa (EB), a group of rare inherited disorders characterised by skin fragility and blistering. The family history, timing of onset, and distribution of lesions are classic for EB.

The condition is caused by autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive inheritance, with mutations in genes encoding structural proteins that maintain dermal-epidermal adhesion varying on the type of mutation. Following skin biopsy, genetic testing should be performed to identify the specific mutation, which is important for genetic counselling and determining prognosis.

The four main types are EB simplex (common), junctional EB (deeper damage), dystrophic EB (blisters and scarring) and Kindler syndrome (with photosensitivity and poikiloderma). Blisters may occur with minor trauma but also can occur in gums, bladder, anus, stomach, nails and eyes. The wounds often can become infected.

Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence mapping is the gold standard investigation for diagnosing and classifying EB. It allows identification of the level of skin cleavage (intraepidermal, junctional, or dermal) and specific protein defects, which is crucial for determining the subtype of EB and guiding management.

Blood culture would be appropriate if there were concerns about infection, but the history and examination findings are more consistent with EB than with staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome or other infectious causes. There is no fever nor other infectious signs.

Serum antinuclear antibody testing is used to investigate autoimmune conditions such as bullous lupus erythematosus. However, the early age of onset and family history make an autoimmune condition unlikely.

Tzanck smear is useful for diagnosing viral infections such as herpes simplex virus. The presentation and family history make a viral cause unlikely in this case.

IgE test would be used to investigate allergic conditions but would not be helpful in diagnosing EB. Elevated IgE levels are associated with atopic conditions but are not relevant to the genetic blistering disorder presented here.
learningPoints: Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence mapping is the gold standard for diagnosing and classifying epidermolysis bullosa.
Genetic testing is important for confirming the specific mutation and enabling genetic counselling.
references: Epidermolysis bullosa. Royal Children's Hospital Dermatology department. 2020. Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/epidermolysis_bullosa/
Ngan V, Widdowson J. Epidermolysis bullosa. DermNet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/epidermolysis-bullosa

Sait H, Srivastava S, Saxena D. Integrated Management Strategies for Epidermolysis Bullosa: Current Insights. Int J Gen Med. 2022 May 24;15:5133-5144. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9148209/
